name: History Facts
subject: Who served as pope three different times?
body:
Benedict IX was an 11th-century Catholic pope notable for being the only person to hold the papacy on multiple occasions — in his case, three different times. [](https://track.historyfacts.com/?xtl=1krv9cfc68cf2osm79oz84w0gqeuc3tafpgff0acy9wzelgxwx5hgptoiwk2bfvo7hgklj6sa7ijpgylautkjcm177g3c14b8htnsjmfbvzzd94phmldcmf1ispuuyki5vsz4v8kh2glos6h742q0tzjf&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com)
---
---
# [Benedict IX served as pope on three different occasions.](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com)
### [World History](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com)
[](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com)
| B enedict IX was an 11th-century Catholic pope notable for being the only person to hold the papacy on multiple occasions — in his case, [three different times](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com). Born Theophylactus to a powerful Roman family, the future pope was the nephew of two prior popes, Benedict VII and John XIX. Upon the latter’s death in 1032, Benedict IX was chosen by the powerful Tusculani family as the successor, despite being just 20 years old (though some sources claim he was as young as 12). Benedict IX’s first pontificate came to an end in 1044, when he was deposed amid accusations of living an unsavory lifestyle, and replaced by Sylvester III.
---
Sylvester’s time as pope was brief, as supporters of Benedict IX expelled Sylvester from the role, allowing Benedict to return in April 1045. But Benedict’s second pontificate lasted only until the following month, as he notoriously sold the papacy to his grandfather Gregory VI. This act landed Gregory in hot water; he was deposed in December 1046 and replaced by Clement II. Clement met an untimely demise a few months later, allowing Benedict to seize the papacy for a third and final time in October 1047. Benedict IX remained pope until July 1048, when he was driven away by forces under the control of Holy Roman Emperor Henry III.
[](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com) | [](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com)

## Benedict XII was accidentally elected pope.
On December 20, 1334, the College of Cardinals gathered to elect a new pope after the death of John XXII. The process of selecting the pope required a two-thirds majority, though it was common for many cardinals to throw away their initial vote on an obscure candidate, as it often took several rounds for a majority to come together around one person. Selecting someone unlikely to win was done in an effort to test the tone of the room and gauge how others were voting before finally coalescing around a winner. This time, however, the strategy backfired, as 11 of 16 cardinals voted for [Jacques Fournier](https://track.historyfacts.com/?&eih=kzm8nstj6prmj7bhz7sjvvnysq73bagh&__stmp=spkc5n&__onlt=h&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com), a candidate deemed to have obscure origins and to lack wealth and followers. Given the rules of papal conclaves, revotes weren’t permitted once a majority had been reached, and so Fournier was thrust into the papacy. He took the papal name Benedict XII and assumed the role on January 8, 1335.

body:

Not only is James ignoring the REAL crimes taking place all over New York, she’s choosing to weaken the President at a time when our nation is in serious decline. Radical Leftists like Tish James do not care about seeing America succeed and prosper. They only care about seeing their political enemies destroyed. Our Republic cannot survive if our justice system continues to be hijacked by legal warfare and political vengeance. There is only one solution – and that is to FIRE Tish James from her position as Attorney General. At Turning Point's AMERICAFEST in December, President Trump stood before thousands of young patriots and declared that a new era of AMERICAN GREATNESS was beginning. On SUNDAY midday, join President Trump's pre-inauguration event, On SUNDAY evening, enjoy VIP tickets to the Turning Point Inaugural Ball, and On MONDAY midday, attend the INAUGURATION of the 47th President in Washington, DC! Plus, you'll receive round-trip flights to Washington, DC and luxury hotel accommodations for inauguration weekend. Don't miss this once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to be part of history, Peter. Thank YOU for your patriotism and your generous support. In 2024, we worked together to inspire MILLIONS MORE kids to LOVE AMERICA again -- and won back an ENTIRE GENERATION in favor of FREEDOM and PATRIOTISM. Now, in 2025, you can help Turning Point USA do more than ever before, starting with this massive celebration of AMERICA'S GOLDEN AGE. ** For America, Charlie Kirk Founder & CEO Turning Point USA 4940 East Beverly Road Phoenix, Arizona 85044

name: MAHA Alliance
subject: Kennedy’s Confirmation: We Need You!
disclaimer_text: Paid for by MAHA Alliance
body:
Every voice matters, and your support drives this pivotal moment.
| | | | | [ ](https://ctrk.klclick2.com/l/01JGRSC05JF0QC55M91FC8TX0Q_0)
---
| | | | Dear Friend, As President Trump nears the culmination of his remarkable political comeback, and as the US Senate prepares to confirm Trump’s cabinet picks, including Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s appointment to lead the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), we have an extraordinary opportunity to ensure a seismic shift in our public health agencies.
2025 is the beginning of a brighter future for the health of every single American.
Simple put, you and I will make the difference. Together, our national grassroots movement to Make America Health Again, and our commitment to transparency, science-based solutions, and prioritizing people over profits is exactly what America needs.
Restore Trust, Accountability, Well-Being
For decades, federal health agencies have operated with zero accountability, putting the health and security of families last while giving precedence to corporate interests.
In just days, those who have abused and misused their authority will be replaced.
WIth the massive mandate given to President Trump, you and I can lean into that and call for bold new reforms. MAHA Alliance is poised to tackle years of mismanagement head-on, bringing compassionate and courageous leadership that prioritizes every American family.
Your Role in this Moment
The momentum is undeniable, and the first step is doing everything we can to ensure Kennedy’s confirmation. That is the start of a movement to transform public health and it will require all of us.
Together, we can show the US Senate and the nation that we are bigger and more powerful than they or their friends in the media know—our fight is about giving Americans a healthcare system they can trust and rely upon.
---
| | | [ ](https://ctrk.klclick2.com/l/01JGRSC05JF0QC55M91FC8TX0Q_1)
---
| | [ DONATE NOW ](https://ctrk.klclick2.com/l/01JGRSC05JF0QC55M91FC8TX0Q_2)
---
| Every voice matters, and your support drives this pivotal moment. [Your donation today](https://ctrk.klclick2.com/l/01JGRSC05JF0QC55M91FC8TX0Q_3) will help amplify Kennedy’s message, counter the forces trying to undermine this progress, and ensure the American people know the truth.
Let’s stand together as we usher in a new era of leadership, transparency, and accountability in public health.
[Click here to take action now >>](https://ctrk.klclick2.com/l/01JGRSC05JF0QC55M91FC8TX0Q_4)
A Better Tomorrow Begins Today
With your support, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will step into this role ready to lead with strength and integrity. Together, we can restore faith in our health system and create a healthier, more hopeful future for generations to come.
This isn’t just a turning point—it’s a victory waiting to be confirmed. Will you join us? With determination, MAHA Alliance

subject: Students still not safe from having their free speech violated
body:
Caught: Another school forces students to comply with DEI| | | [](http://links.freedomtrainalert.com/u/click?_t=2d4f229f156142619b4e4f956d2d65c9&_m=eacb4041404f43d09e27cf9bd89f0105&_e=2Hrihz31CGRSdRI1JQTV_011cto1UrJr_MPV5wPF3vhB3QjXbwNd1Ab0LEMEXRS6EkTcbDAD-HaXvokMf9yT3gcBLENFCvvrspjMaPtHuzH9gfEV_hAuEdWTiWAIqVH5bo3vXXY4n6CUp3e2Br3ryQ6kXuFiLnt5jsi6Vu7e6dFC3HKw5pucFr2x9lixawjtfRsqpD2VRq1bcPKpfGEdioR5kTgBCEDylwI7zmJY_tCOpM0r9Ic3zRpOdbUObr6mfGabWvFTGc_E7rqs806JYhC3wQ-m137pSSLpwI6_Guk%3D)
---
Peter, Many people think university administrators will stop abusing their authority to force their ideology on students because Donald Trump was elected president. But the radical elites in higher education won't give up their ground that easily. _Now is the time to root out DEI requirements that have been embedded into campus curriculum at universities across the country._ For example, it was recently reported that Elon University is trying to create "equity-minded leaders" by forcing early education majors to do social-justice projects as part of a core class.
---
[](http://links.freedomtrainalert.com/u/click?_t=2d4f229f156142619b4e4f956d2d65c9&_m=eacb4041404f43d09e27cf9bd89f0105&_e=2Hrihz31CGRSdRI1JQTV_011cto1UrJr_MPV5wPF3vhB3QjXbwNd1Ab0LEMEXRS6EkTcbDAD-HaXvokMf9yT3gcBLENFCvvrspjMaPtHuzH9gfEV_hAuEdWTiWAIqVH5bo3vXXY4n6CUp3e2Br3ryRIgxHVIdThSnV5Ln6Hv_5_PODol6KZzfMiObiyI-cpt42HZbTjrlgJ1drfD9nZTZNi6afMQn9ZDD1ynmNWy7u9YltbRSSe1O7gecXO-CJaFFy6xlMMMEqiObBv1Y0KS82EOA67LcyrloRkfhR5HA0s%3D)

Speech First has a proven track record of exposing the WORST violations of students' First Amendment rights on campus. Earlier this year, a brave student used our DEI and Indoctrination Tip Line to reveal that her school, the University of South Carolina, mandated that students affirm the value of DEI programs in order to register for classes. _But we do more than just gin up headlines when administrators overstep their authority._ **In just five years, we 've won seven major free speech cases against universities, setting precedents in dozens of states.**
---
[](http://links.freedomtrainalert.com/u/click?_t=2d4f229f156142619b4e4f956d2d65c9&_m=eacb4041404f43d09e27cf9bd89f0105&_e=2Hrihz31CGRSdRI1JQTV_011cto1UrJr_MPV5wPF3vhB3QjXbwNd1Ab0LEMEXRS6EkTcbDAD-HaXvokMf9yT3gcBLENFCvvrspjMaPtHuzH9gfEV_hAuEdWTiWAIqVH5bo3vXXY4n6CUp3e2Br3rydluLp3U2hehlirIlax1onOmZhOAkEwNuMrRrxgz32YH9mLiFBys1tmOk0knXJqK94ZKqP6ppPOWtUdhDGoF6BF9ScKSG7FTttNp8CuilQYNNKtk_1c9MkXdOR9krtd9Qrw8EzJIay-0wfdaseO6Yxk%3D)

We've ended unconstitutional policies like bias response teams and speech codes at schools like University of Michigan, University of Texas, and University of Central Florida. And we're currently battling Indiana University and Texas State University in court for their attempts to chill student speech - and with your help, _we 'll win_. _**[Will you join Speech First in the fight for students' free speech rights with your generous gift of $25, $50, or $100 today?](http://links.freedomtrainalert.com/u/click?_t=2d4f229f156142619b4e4f956d2d65c9&_m=eacb4041404f43d09e27cf9bd89f0105&_e=2Hrihz31CGRSdRI1JQTV_011cto1UrJr_MPV5wPF3vhB3QjXbwNd1Ab0LEMEXRS6EkTcbDAD-HaXvokMf9yT3gcBLENFCvvrspjMaPtHuzH9gfEV_hAuEdWTiWAIqVH5bo3vXXY4n6CUp3e2Br3ryRADsGWbfJx97MJeVaraljUukmwXJUVdGvY_IUCSXkXsc33ECYrnEexzVeaTy8aseHRs3hbFGxQHwCAZmeGAxW_8OxzuiRu-9YM7j4xaVsC_LrpSCIWc7Zq6neXz39BKDKs9t1UHtJWnhy_Rle9cXeY%3D)**_

---
Future generations will be worse off if we allow DEI programs and other forms of toxic censorship to affect their education._Imagine what all those "equity-minded" students will teach their future kindergartners instead of reading, writing, and arithmetic._ **If we don 't defend a culture of free speech on campus, the leaders of tomorrow will go out into the world and pass on these poisonous ideas to even younger generations.** _**[But together we can stop unconstitutional, anti-speech policies in their tracks. Will you give any amount you're able today to join Speech First in protecting students' 1A rights?](http://links.freedomtrainalert.com/u/click?_t=2d4f229f156142619b4e4f956d2d65c9&_m=eacb4041404f43d09e27cf9bd89f0105&_e=2Hrihz31CGRSdRI1JQTV_011cto1UrJr_MPV5wPF3vhB3QjXbwNd1Ab0LEMEXRS6EkTcbDAD-HaXvokMf9yT3gcBLENFCvvrspjMaPtHuzH9gfEV_hAuEdWTiWAIqVH5bo3vXXY4n6CUp3e2Br3ryZvplt2QN0ud3u_S7kTChYDpee2_uveJtXkl0z0nsMHtx1fcpL-OXc4sqleqWCOcrMPHIjBD02s7FAW3mvTwe8FNpqOyxPAm4nEN2MWZmon1wCCL2sobiSbhthz-XpsC29CYeLlmRpZ2GlY3A2L5wdA%3D)**_

---
Every day, more studies come out showing that DEI and other social justice initiatives on campus only increase hostility and division. Yet colleges and universities continue to plow millions of taxpayer dollars into these programs, **forcing students to comply with them by holding their registrations, grades, and diplomas hostage.** _As the only organization in America solely focused on defending students and their free speech rights, we 're in a unique position to defeat these schools in court time and again._ We have, and we will - and I hope you'll partner with us today in driving these victories on behalf of college students across the country with your generous gift today.

clean_body: Caught: Another school forces students to comply with DEI| | | Peter, Many people think university administrators will stop abusing their authority to force their ideology on students because Donald Trump was elected president. But the radical elites in higher education won't give up their ground that easily. Now is the time to root out DEI requirements that have been embedded into campus curriculum at universities across the country. For example, it was recently reported that Elon University is trying to create "equity-minded leaders" by forcing early education majors to do social-justice projects as part of a core class. Speech First has a proven track record of exposing the WORST violations of students' First Amendment rights on campus. Earlier this year, a brave student used our DEI and Indoctrination Tip Line to reveal that her school, the University of South Carolina, mandated that students affirm the value of DEI programs in order to register for classes. But we do more than just gin up headlines when administrators overstep their authority. In just five years, we 've won seven major free speech cases against universities, setting precedents in dozens of states. We've ended unconstitutional policies like bias response teams and speech codes at schools like University of Michigan, University of Texas, and University of Central Florida. And we're currently battling Indiana University and Texas State University in court for their attempts to chill student speech - and with your help, we 'll win. Future generations will be worse off if we allow DEI programs and other forms of toxic censorship to affect their education.Imagine what all those "equity-minded" students will teach their future kindergartners instead of reading, writing, and arithmetic. If we don 't defend a culture of free speech on campus, the leaders of tomorrow will go out into the world and pass on these poisonous ideas to even younger generations. Every day, more studies come out showing that DEI and other social justice initiatives on campus only increase hostility and division. Yet colleges and universities continue to plow millions of taxpayer dollars into these programs, forcing students to comply with them by holding their registrations, grades, and diplomas hostage. As the only organization in America solely focused on defending students and their free speech rights, we 're in a unique position to defeat these schools in court time and again. We have, and we will - and I hope you'll partner with us today in driving these victories on behalf of college students across the country with your generous gift today. Together, we can counter toxic censorship culture with a spirit of free speech and equal rights on campus. Thank you for standing with Speech First in this fight. Cherise Trump Executive DirectorSpeech First 1300 I St NW Suite 400EWashington, DC 20005

name: Jeremiah Regan
subject: C.S. Lewis’s Profound Insights
body:
Access “C.S. Lewis on Christianity”

_**A message from our sponsor...**_ | | | [](http://links.bestamericanow.com/u/click?_t=f6e78b69acbb4291bd75bc93fae35a62&_m=c47afc6b88df477fa4a30289f56af2a3&_e=_2pcuXV2GdJxJQt8E6_K-w5AgOvmsoa-FNeM78aFPqWH3hO2JiMRAjiJ8ANd8-iM5GZ_3Qr9Ws7jgq61vFzJhR7tSCHZJZshJe1bOfXMHPM-4f6XKkKajtSXPXgNR7RjoEyb3TE_TcvgnUozL4x5-iGmu6M99NPt_8JSh2YZBkIApUrdVLrVC0P3OC-phsW8VDxlMQYp2tVCFiUrN03STlEDEDWEm05uLnosppgXCOTrthdG34rlcWciVHPJGlJ-1wdOwR4nKkfNFJsyDZXyYgyR2X14eLIQz61DJU9w-S2u2jKwYBDfkBsu7ApZ_Icj9nxoWOYgp4Pfzfz4A16aH5MmBuTqI9KD821A7QFePUQJABK9mIWjJmPTldoc3PJ9zxbCjbA_6SYfgHdxR7r56jKFKcM8oGNScSVLn6V9KjIUDnwFT9ZEMxCilwm71iNQ)
---
| | Dear Peter, C.S. Lewis was a master at addressing life's most important questions through vivid language that is delightful and captivating to read.

His signature ability to communicate the truth about Christianity has inspired countless skeptics to become believers and even more believers to renew and deepen their faith--making him the greatest Christian apologist of the twentieth century.

To help you learn more about Lewis, we produced "[**C.S. Lewis on Christianity,**](http://links.bestamericanow.com/u/click?_t=f6e78b69acbb4291bd75bc93fae35a62&_m=c47afc6b88df477fa4a30289f56af2a3&_e=_2pcuXV2GdJxJQt8E6_K-w5AgOvmsoa-FNeM78aFPqWH3hO2JiMRAjiJ8ANd8-iM5GZ_3Qr9Ws7jgq61vFzJhR7tSCHZJZshJe1bOfXMHPM-4f6XKkKajtSXPXgNR7RjoEyb3TE_TcvgnUozL4x5-iGmu6M99NPt_8JSh2YZBkIApUrdVLrVC0P3OC-phsW8VDxlMQYp2tVCFiUrN03STlEDEDWEm05uLnosppgXCOTrthdG34rlcWciVHPJGlJ-1wdOwR4nKkfNFJsyDZXyYgyR2X14eLIQz61DJU9w-S3fChfWUXhECR1KaSn00ZzZBZ2kPJ9iVNp0xtcHlgBhnsGfTbpDzFWJ2HFWow5cDxx8vhLjmmlpkDDkI--df5JUNdfwSL4l9TZvV3KhpQFt5YXRLanG0aVLP3cuxgcM5k8XJFzJ58Vc2tTsomQeI0fB)" which you can now own on DVD.
---
| [](http://links.bestamericanow.com/u/click?_t=f6e78b69acbb4291bd75bc93fae35a62&_m=c47afc6b88df477fa4a30289f56af2a3&_e=_2pcuXV2GdJxJQt8E6_K-w5AgOvmsoa-FNeM78aFPqWH3hO2JiMRAjiJ8ANd8-iM5GZ_3Qr9Ws7jgq61vFzJhR7tSCHZJZshJe1bOfXMHPM-4f6XKkKajtSXPXgNR7RjoEyb3TE_TcvgnUozL4x5-iGmu6M99NPt_8JSh2YZBkIApUrdVLrVC0P3OC-phsW8VDxlMQYp2tVCFiUrN03STlEDEDWEm05uLnosppgXCOTrthdG34rlcWciVHPJGlJ-1wdOwR4nKkfNFJsyDZXyYgyR2X14eLIQz61DJU9w-S3_VaUrWiM0R5rszQ-rj2V4lTGFkd3zCeXHY20tnkqsxvE4dmrYer_G_S0PQO0RMmsTKpDHYQtzDlxxkoOlrBF_N13-PE6KjjbhfIjilSGTT-j8WhGcW3wzP20YmOjwODMlCOmCgubTSE9DEomlZy7o)
---
| | In this course you will learn Lewis's profound insights on the central topics of Christianity, including:
* good and evil,
* conversion and faith,
* prayer and the Bible,
* suffering and death,
* heaven and hell, and much more.

The instructor, Dr. Michael Ward, is a Distinguished Visiting Professor at Hillsdale College and a member of the Faculty of Theology and Religion at the University of Oxford. He is the author of _Planet Narnia: The Seven Heavens in the Imagination of C.S. Lewis_ and _After Humanity: A Guide to C.S. Lewis 's The Abolition of Man_, and the co-editor of _The Cambridge Companion to C.S. Lewis_. He even spent three years living in Lewis's Oxford home as the resident Warden of The Kilns!

This course is especially moving because it examines C.S. Lewis's beautiful writings about faith and traces Lewis's own journey from skeptic to believer. The combination provides an opportunity for us to grow in our knowledge and understanding of Christianity.

That's why I hope you'll join us in examining the eternal questions of Christianity with C.S. Lewis with [this beautiful DVD box set](http://links.bestamericanow.com/u/click?_t=f6e78b69acbb4291bd75bc93fae35a62&_m=c47afc6b88df477fa4a30289f56af2a3&_e=_2pcuXV2GdJxJQt8E6_K-w5AgOvmsoa-FNeM78aFPqWH3hO2JiMRAjiJ8ANd8-iM5GZ_3Qr9Ws7jgq61vFzJhR7tSCHZJZshJe1bOfXMHPM-4f6XKkKajtSXPXgNR7RjoEyb3TE_TcvgnUozL4x5-iGmu6M99NPt_8JSh2YZBkIApUrdVLrVC0P3OC-phsW8VDxlMQYp2tVCFiUrN03STlEDEDWEm05uLnosppgXCOTrthdG34rlcWciVHPJGlJ-1wdOwR4nKkfNFJsyDZXyYgyR2X14eLIQz61DJU9w-S15j1bBQEUL12GuMBq5elmEbUB9Jy9nKuvMD6F1Z-B0B8ELw8MegxJhfGAma35YhLJ1OARxSxsU36ltJzy411GbkBnDwyv880JCT8ejbSxeDII5590RBydnSShiHz8jOJ9L0_QDJ3GxZS4s-Xp3gynV).

Best regards,

Jeremiah Regan, Ph.D.
Executive Director of Online Learning
Hillsdale College [Unsubscribe](http://links.bestamericanow.com/e/encryptedUnsubscribe?_r=f6e78b69acbb4291bd75bc93fae35a62&_s=c47afc6b88df477fa4a30289f56af2a3&_t=1s5OJomfD5aMpNbPHiOHBFTnwQXWU0cfiBLiB6UzUGngK2izLflJGXMMBcx-L5J1QHSeQEECA5x6U9WcbHOdnEDvFfge03Wf4LoEHs3wZpd8gB60HfaVJIrNqV9q7WfM)

name: Vicki Daguerre-Bradford
subject: The most important weekend
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Democratic Party of Virginia, www
body:
Your support this weekend could decide the outcome of these special elections

| [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/99832261/521004836/-793144256?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDYvMS84NDIzNSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3YTBlNTM4Ny1hOWNhLWVmMTEtODhkMC0wMDIyNDgyYTliNDUiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=bZdOlNwYO9MfKnLGBDRErSoXExCIE6-XVl4TfBid7wo=&emci=609f144b-15ca-ef11-88d0-0022482a9b45&emdi=7a0e5387-a9ca-ef11-88d0-0022482a9b45&ceid=15759664)
---
Derek,As Finance Director for DPVA, I see firsthand how every dollar raised directly impacts our ability to win elections. And now, with**just three days left** until the special elections on Tuesday, I cannot overstate the importance of this moment. This is our last opportunity to knock on doors, make phone calls, and remind voters to head to the polls. **But doing this work requires resources like printed materials, supplies for volunteers, getting reminders in people’s inboxes, and more.** [_**Every grassroots contribution – whether it’s $5, $25, or $50 – means we can support one more volunteer, print one more walk card, or reach one more voter. Derek, I set a goal to raise $1,500 per day to support these critical special elections, and we’re falling short of today’s goal.**_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/99832262/521004837/-1953633157?&refcode=E11894-20250104-LastWeekend-SL-GOTV&amount=25&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDYvMS84NDIzNSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3YTBlNTM4Ny1hOWNhLWVmMTEtODhkMC0wMDIyNDgyYTliNDUiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=bZdOlNwYO9MfKnLGBDRErSoXExCIE6-XVl4TfBid7wo=) | **************[HELP US GOTV](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/99832263/521004838/-1757377202?refcode=E11894-20250104-LastWeekend-SB-GOTV&amount=25&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDYvMS84NDIzNSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3YTBlNTM4Ny1hOWNhLWVmMTEtODhkMC0wMDIyNDgyYTliNDUiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=bZdOlNwYO9MfKnLGBDRErSoXExCIE6-XVl4TfBid7wo=)**************
---
The work we do this weekend will make the difference between victory and defeat. I’ve seen campaigns win or lose by just a weekend of work – this is why your support is so crucial, to ensure we have what we need to hustle and work as hard as we can in these final days.
[_**Please. Derek, rush a donation today if you’re able.**_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/99832264/521004839/-1953633157?&refcode=E11894-20250104-LastWeekend-SL-GOTV&amount=25&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDYvMS84NDIzNSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3YTBlNTM4Ny1hOWNhLWVmMTEtODhkMC0wMDIyNDgyYTliNDUiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=bZdOlNwYO9MfKnLGBDRErSoXExCIE6-XVl4TfBid7wo=)
We can’t leave anything to chance. Together, we’ll turn out as many Democratic voters as we can and secure the wins Virginia needs.
Thank you for being a part of this fight.
Vicki Daguerre-Bradford
Finance Director
Team DPVA

## Johnson is 2 votes short of being elected House Speaker in 1st round * WorldNetDaily * by Bob Unruh
House of Representatives House Speaker Mike Johnson missed being re-elected to the leadership post, third in line to the presidency, by only two votes early Friday, with another vote pending soon. Johnson, who was handed the gavel two years ago when Speaker Kevin McCarthy was removed by his own party, had to endure multiple votes [...] [Read more](http://links.conservativeum.com/u/click?_t=26c2e4aa9bbe40418f61e26972138f21&_m=4bbacaa84dc74afab0c1ed964ff17282&_e=oxGHWF4C5vBqIMXLyAu6dpOodsewNcuxtf8SGUG4T7N0yeJjFZkOAtS69NXA5tXIBS1NqOq9Eqwajzac_AdPzSYLq0xNE3HIf6xILHk-0Y_MFlX83WP6i2Wxdt-poJ70w_JScFfsOiVKjOFYr0PDh0nobWwZ7HLV9SSOWwZfpcTazpJDuo4No3Zy-LCZoqe9BzJnteR0rk6d7gMg4gN8BxtvGhVCYT0ZcLHkfPazIC1dHAX3jA3o8effkbCJRNup7XoyOJ4M73ItED5DNzOTLgnTcu7DzSvlUO854kTzCzczt70F86mzL2W2s2Y3AVu371Y5rirET73TEnzoyxJFkA%3D%3D)

The city of New Orleans' motto seems to have always been "Laissez les bon temps rouler" — ("Let the good times roll.") It is America's party capital. Therefore, you would think they would have crowd control and management down to the same science that Floridians have for hurricane recovery. The grapple for the speaker’s gavel is headed to round two after three Republicans withheld their votes from House Speaker Mike Johnson, potentially pushing Johnson’s speakership to the brink of collapse. Three Republican lawmakers defected from the party leadership choosing to endorse three alternative candidates. They include Rep. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., who voted for Rep. ## Speaker Mike Johnson Commits to Working With the DOGE
While Speaker Mike Johnson's hold on the gavel is being decided, the man himself is making some interesting commitments. On Friday morning, Speaker Johnson took to X to state one such, that being to work with Elon Musk, Vivek Ramaswamy, and the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to hopefully get federal spending under control. _" If something of importance is known in Texas, Matt knows it. With a decline in the number of credible news organizations, the Must Read Texas morning email is indispensable for anyone that wants to continue to be informed." - Former U.S. Senator **Phil Gramm** (R-TX)_
* * *
**AUSTIN NEWSLETTER LAUNCHED**
Are you one of the 100,000+ subscribers to [ATX Pulse](https://substack.com/redirect/496e02a5-054b-4576-8002-7cac46ec4655?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), a newsletter that delivers EVERYTHING you NEED TO KNOW about Austin?
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/badd932a-27f3-4fd8-aa46-9c6852b2ae40?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|

**» Read this morning's (free) edition **[here](https://substack.com/redirect/8957edb6-772f-486a-9f74-ea8701248ef3?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
>> SUBSCRIBE HERE: [ATXpulsepremium.com](https://substack.com/redirect/496e02a5-054b-4576-8002-7cac46ec4655?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). ($5/mo or $50/yr or $199 lifetime)
* * *
* **TOP NEWS**

"**Texas Supreme Court to review Winter Storm Uri claims against utility companies,** " Texas Lawbook's **Janet Elliott** -- "With state electric grid operators eyeing the potential for extreme cold this winter, the [Texas Supreme Court ](https://substack.com/redirect/b79a5176-9388-4dda-bf66-aaffea2169b9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)has agreed to hear a petition from transmission and distribution utility companies seeking dismissal of gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims over devastating winter storm [blackouts](https://substack.com/redirect/4e929be0-efc4-4d05-b3d2-23029b12674b?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) in February 2021.

Justices will hear arguments Feb. 19, four years after [Winter Storm Uri caused widespread, prolonged power outages](https://substack.com/redirect/9878af12-2e5b-4358-b0fb-276b4ce61ee0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) resulting in deaths and property damage.

The court will review an [April decision by a three-judge panel of the Fourteenth Court of Appeals in Houston](https://substack.com/redirect/b2cb8803-c677-464f-a408-ea7ac7222fe8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that more than 15,000 plaintiffs may move forward with certain claims in their wrongful death, personal injury and other Uri-related lawsuits seeking billions of dollars in damages.

The Fourteenth Court said the utility companies were entitled to dismissal of the plaintiffs' claims for negligence because Texas law does not impose common-law negligence duties associated with emergency power interruptions, ensuring adequate generation or warning of anticipated power outages.
[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $7/mo](https://substack.com/redirect/13161a03-a48d-46ce-a452-1fdf1a696bb1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $70/yr](https://substack.com/redirect/13161a03-a48d-46ce-a452-1fdf1a696bb1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
But the court of appeals said that the tariff, which is essentially the state-law mandated contract between customers and the transmission and distribution companies, does allow for gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims.

More than 200 lawsuits, consolidated into multidistrict litigation before Harris County MDL Court Judge **Sylvia Matthews** , allege that mismanagement by companies including Dallas-based Oncor Electric Delivery, CenterPoint Energy in Houston and American Electric Power constituted an intentional nuisance.

Uri was a perfect storm for the electric grid that serves most of Texas as plunging temperatures sparked record high demand amid a severely reduced energy supply, causing the grid to become unbalanced. Beginning at 1:25 a.m. on Feb. 15, 2021, [ERCOT began ordering](https://substack.com/redirect/b854b652-c7bf-4c28-bbed-a9df104646c9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) the utilities to cut service to set amounts of customer demand to prevent the grid from collapsing.

Millions of Texans lost heat and power for several days as the weather remained brutally cold. News reports have attributed nearly 250 deaths to the disaster.

The transmission and distribution companies say they could be the only category of the many entities involved in the crisis to have a legal duty to deliver electricity after court rulings in other Uri-related cases went in favor of natural gas providers, power generators, retail electric providers and ERCOT defendants.

Michael A. Heidler, an appellate partner at Vinson & Elkins, said in the companies' petition for review that the Supreme Court should bar the plaintiffs' claims before significant resources are spent adjudicating "15,000 claimants' baseless complaints."

"In these exigent circumstances, no court has imposed common-law duties on TDUs [transmission and distribution utilities] to supply electricity to any particular customers for any particular duration. Established Texas law confirms that courts should not impose such a duty in these circumstances. Plaintiffs' gross-negligence claim should be dismissed," Heidler wrote.

He said the companies did not create a nuisance by shedding load in response to ERCOT's orders. Citing a 2016 Texas Supreme Court pipeline case, Heidler said that "nuisance" is a condition such as smoke, gases or odors that substantially interferes with use and enjoyment of land.

"Here, the 'condition' interfering with use and enjoyment of land was cold air. But it was Mother Nature -- not TDUs -- that blew cold air onto Plaintiffs' properties," Heidler said.

In a response for plaintiff Bernadine Edwards, Dallas lawyer S. Ann Saucer said the utilities are not being sued for complying with ERCOT's load shed orders but because they committed malfeasance that ERCOT did not order.

"TDUs are not being sued because they failed to deliver uninterrupted adequate power but because they, either intentionally or via gross negligence, created part of the power shortfall, told untruths to their customers, and then deprived their misled customers of power for undue stretches of time rather than roll blackouts, with catastrophic results including deaths," said Saucer, who is with Nachawati Law Group.

The utility companies are being supported with briefs from the industry and other business groups." [Texas Lawbook](https://substack.com/redirect/c78a8c07-e751-49ef-b8ca-ca73a72befbf?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)

"**Texas' grid could connect to New Mexico if company's request is approved,** " Dallas Morning News' **Philip Jankowski** -- "A small regional electric cooperative in far West Texas is seeking to connect portions of New Mexico to Texas' self-contained power grid.

The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative, a rural electric retail company that owns thousands of miles of power lines across 18 Texas counties, is not wholly part of ERCOT, but it wants to be.

Last month, the power provider petitioned the federal regulatory body overseeing the nation's electric grid and asked regulators to determine whether connecting their customers to ERCOT, including homes and businesses in two New Mexico counties, would trigger federal regulation.

Texas has maintained an energy grid largely disconnected from the rest of the country. This isolation has allowed the state to avoid federal regulation, which many lawmakers, including Gov. **Greg Abbott** , have touted as an economic advantage contributing to the state's sustained economic growth.

The electric cooperative argues allowing ERCOT power to cross state lines would not trigger federal regulation because all the large electric transmission lines they own are in Texas. The electricity that crosses state lines is transmitted on smaller distribution lines like those connected to homes and small businesses across Texas. They argue that the Federal Electric Regulatory Commission does not have jurisdiction over those lines.

The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative's CEO **Theresa Quiroz** said in an interview that allowing the co-op to connect its remaining customers to ERCOT was "critical" to the organization's ability to meet future power needs in its coverage area.

"We have a lot of entities inquiring of us whether we have the capacity and ability to serve them," Quiroz said. "We have an obligation to serve them. However, we can't do it with what we have in place. At this point, we are having to turn away customers, and we don't want to do that."

No hearings have been scheduled in the case, but the Rio Grande Electric Cooperative asked federal regulators to issue a ruling by Feb. 24." [DMN](https://substack.com/redirect/ef574d4d-3ce2-4004-abe1-6a0a6e5289bd?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Medicaid whistleblowers awarded $37 million from state in 13-year-old case against Xeroz,** " Austin American-Statesman's **John C. Moritz** -- "The state of Texas has been ordered to pay three whistleblowers more than $37 million in a 13-year-old case relating to Medicaid fraud alleging improper billings by dentists and corporate dental chains.

State District Judge **Maya Guerra Gamble** of Travis County on Dec. 19 handed down the award, which amounts to 17.5% of a record 2019 settlement of more than $200 million the state reached with Xerox State Healthcare, formerly known as ACS State Healthcare.

According to a news release from the lawyers who reresented whistleblowers **Alexandra Alvarez** , **Joshua LaFountain** and **Christine Ellis** , the state had settled the lawsuit but balked at paying a percentage of the award proceeds to those who had brought the allegations to light as required by a provision in a state fraud prevention law.

"Because of these three courageous whistleblowers, the State of Texas ultimately showed how Xerox/ACS, Inc. deeply compromised the integrity of the Texas Medicaid program," attorney**James "Rusty" Tucker** said in the release. "Their efforts to set the record straight on the practices of corporate dental practices, led to a historic recovery by the state and will help protect taxpayers going forward."

The lawyers said Ellis, an orthodontist, had observed what she believed to be fraud in orthodontic billings under the state's Medicaid program and reported it to state authorities and to a congressional committee. Ellis, Alvarez and LaFountain each filed a lawsuit in 2012 making the allegations under the Texas Medicaid Fraud Prevention Act.

The act contains a provision that whistleblowers are entitled to a share of any recovery resulting from their suits, according to the lawyers. The Texas attorney general's office intervened in the lawsuits and later filed its own legal action against Xerox.

When Texas Attorney General **Ken Paxton** [announced the settlement in](https://substack.com/redirect/40e53a04-1b73-4479-a0a1-236b95e0c033?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) February 2019, his office said in a news release that Xerox and its affiliated companies "rubber-stamped orthodontic prior authorization requests" without properly reviewing each one. Under state law, only requests that meet "strict Medicaid program requirements" are allowable under Medicaid.

"Misconduct by employees of Xerox and its related companies compromised the integrity of the Medicaid program - the very program Texas hired the Xerox defendants to safeguard through the administration of a proper prior authorization review," Paxton said in the 2019 release, making no mention of the whistleblowers who originally raised the allegations. "We're proud of this recovery of taxpayer money. My office is committed to ensuring that Medicaid dollars are preserved for those who need it most."" [AAS](https://substack.com/redirect/6eee8174-9bdb-463b-b68b-0ffaf9028da7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

* **BUSINESS NEWS**

"**Inside high-pressure sales culture of Texas' solar energy boom,** " San Antonio Express-News'**Sara DiNatale** -- "**Ian Atkinson** began with a question: What should you look for when you're out knocking on doors early in the day, searching for customers?

"No. 1: senior citizens," he said.

Atkinson was standing at a dry-erase board in an office building in Arlington, leading a training session for solar energy sales agents. Sales of residential rooftop solar systems had shot up across Texas after [Winter Storm Uri exposed the ricketiness of the state's electric grid](https://substack.com/redirect/d91f83a9-d8a0-4da6-acda-4fe140a88efb?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Atkinson's students wanted to get in on the action.

As one of the trainees recorded video of the session, Atkinson recited tips for spotting prospects and closing deals. He ticked off telltale signs of an elderly person's home: a Buick or Oldsmobile in the driveway, neat lawns decorated with pinwheels or garden gnomes.

People with gewgaws in the yard "always buy stuff," he said.

He exhorted the agents to "kneel for the deal" -- get down on eye-level with a senior seated in a recliner. Speak slowly. Answer questions patiently. Pretend you're talking to your grandparents.

"They might be on a fixed income where it's only Social Security or retirement," Atkinson can be heard saying, "but they usually have passing credit."

Door-to-door sales agents were the engine behind the explosive growth of residential solar across Texas -- and they're blamed for [many of its excesses](https://substack.com/redirect/8e30252d-a006-4a39-b6ac-b62c9c9741cb?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Some of them promised customers big energy savings that never materialized, rebates that didn't exist and tax benefits they didn't necessarily qualify for, a [San Antonio Express-News investigation](https://substack.com/redirect/8e30252d-a006-4a39-b6ac-b62c9c9741cb?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) found.

Sales agents also collected big commissions that were rarely disclosed to the homeowners who wound up paying them.

Customers who signed for no-money-down loans to finance solar systems took salespeople at their word that the rooftop panels would generate all their home's power needs, slashing their electric bills to zero. When that proved untrue, customers were left with loan payments stretching out for decades -- on top of their utility bills.

Many systems worked poorly or not at all because of flawed design or shoddy workmanship.

These conclusions emerge from dozens of interviews and a review of court records, written sales training materials, training videos, agents' group text threads, inspection reports and other documents. In addition, the Express-News reviewed more than 1,000 complaints about home solar systems filed with the Texas Office of the Attorney General from January 2023 through April 2024.

In 83% of those cases, customers said they'd been misled by salespeople. The victims were often elderly, ill and disabled or had limited command of English." [SAEN](https://substack.com/redirect/f942dbab-ade0-4bb4-8b37-fbba3182a805?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

* **TRUMP ADMINISTRATION**

"**Trump's 'border czar' has ties to Texas. Here's what to know,** " Houston Chronicle's**Benjamin Wermund** -- "The man President-elect **Donald Trump** tapped to oversee mass deportations [is a close ally](https://substack.com/redirect/80c04d6b-d5d9-4506-bfe4-a874a934ad36?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) of Gov.**Greg Abbott** who developed some of his hardline stances while working as an immigration enforcement officer in Texas.

**Tom Homan** has vowed to launch what Trump has cast as the "largest deportation operation in the history of our country" that will start day one of his presidency.

"Let me be clear: there is going to be a mass deportation because we just finished a mass illegal immigration crisis on the border," Homan said during a trip to the border with Abbott in November. He added the administration was "already planning" the operation.

"We're not waiting until January to secure this nation at the highest levels this nation has ever seen," Homan said.

Homan has yet to lay out many details. But experts say it would be expensive and next to impossible to carry out mass removals under the current immigration system.

Homan, who worked as a New York police officer before climbing the ranks of federal immigration enforcement, led Immigrations and Customs Enforcement during Trump's first term. He was an early proponent of separating migrant parents from their children to discourage border crossings and has cheered on Abbott's efforts to install floating barriers in the Rio Grande and line the banks with thick strands of razor wire.

Trump appointed Homan in an advisory role, meaning he won't need Senate confirmation to take the job.

Here's what else to know about the "border czar" set to guide Trump's border policies.

**Texas shaped Homan's border security views**

Starting in 1999, Homan oversaw investigations for the Immigration and Naturalization Service, an agency that would eventually be folded into the Department of Homeland Security, and did stints in San Antonio and Dallas.

Homan said an experience working a human smuggling case in Victoria helped shape his desire for stricter immigration enforcement.

In that 2003 case, a truck driver bringing migrants into the country illegally reportedly cut A/C to their tractor trailer to evade suspicion at a border checkpoint. In the stifling heat, 19 migrants died.

Homan was called to the scene, which he described in gruesome detail in a book detailing the decades he spent working for federal immigration enforcement.

"If you saw what I saw that day, you would understand why I'm so emotional about border security, illegal immigration and national policy," he wrote. "Secure borders strangle human smugglers, drug traffickers and cartels... That's what this battle on the border is all about."

His opposition to so-called "sanctuary cities" also has roots in Texas, when his agency arrested undocumented immigrants in a raid on Austin nightclubs suspected to be involved in drug dealing and prostitution in the early 2000s.

Local police were uncomfortable assisting federal immigration authorities, he wrote in the book, and later the chief, whom he did not name, said the department would not be helping with immigration enforcement.

"I guess this was a sign of things to come in liberal cities," he wrote, "pandering to certain voter constituencies while violating their oath and abandoning the most vulnerable in immigrant communities and the very citizens they're supposed to protect."

Homan's work in Texas put him on the radar of the director of the then-newly formed Immigration and Customs Enforcement, and eventually led him to Washington.

During the Obama administration, Homan oversaw deportations when they ramped up to more than 2.5 million -- setting records and leading immigrant groups to derisively refer to Barack Obama as "Deporter in Chief."" [Houston Chronicle](https://substack.com/redirect/8b248cab-8653-46ad-8e77-640352d92ab8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

($5/mo or $50/yr or $199 lifetime) TOP NEWS "Texas Supreme Court to review Winter Storm Uri claims against utility companies, " Texas Lawbook's Janet Elliott -- "With state electric grid operators eyeing the potential for extreme cold this winter, the agreed to hear a petition from transmission and distribution utility companies seeking dismissal of gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims over devastating winter storm in February 2021. Justices will hear arguments Feb. 19, four years after resulting in deaths and property damage. The court will review an that more than 15,000 plaintiffs may move forward with certain claims in their wrongful death, personal injury and other Uri-related lawsuits seeking billions of dollars in damages. The Fourteenth Court said the utility companies were entitled to dismissal of the plaintiffs' claims for negligence because Texas law does not impose common-law negligence duties associated with emergency power interruptions, ensuring adequate generation or warning of anticipated power outages. But the court of appeals said that the tariff, which is essentially the state-law mandated contract between customers and the transmission and distribution companies, does allow for gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims. More than 200 lawsuits, consolidated into multidistrict litigation before Harris County MDL Court Judge Sylvia Matthews , allege that mismanagement by companies including Dallas-based Oncor Electric Delivery, CenterPoint Energy in Houston and American Electric Power constituted an intentional nuisance. Uri was a perfect storm for the electric grid that serves most of Texas as plunging temperatures sparked record high demand amid a severely reduced energy supply, causing the grid to become unbalanced. Beginning at 1:25 a.m. on Feb. 15, 2021, the utilities to cut service to set amounts of customer demand to prevent the grid from collapsing. Millions of Texans lost heat and power for several days as the weather remained brutally cold. News reports have attributed nearly 250 deaths to the disaster. The transmission and distribution companies say they could be the only category of the many entities involved in the crisis to have a legal duty to deliver electricity after court rulings in other Uri-related cases went in favor of natural gas providers, power generators, retail electric providers and ERCOT defendants. Michael A. Heidler, an appellate partner at Vinson & Elkins, said in the companies' petition for review that the Supreme Court should bar the plaintiffs' claims before significant resources are spent adjudicating "15,000 claimants' baseless complaints." "In these exigent circumstances, no court has imposed common-law duties on TDUs [transmission and distribution utilities] to supply electricity to any particular customers for any particular duration. Established Texas law confirms that courts should not impose such a duty in these circumstances. Plaintiffs' gross-negligence claim should be dismissed," Heidler wrote. He said the companies did not create a nuisance by shedding load in response to ERCOT's orders. Citing a 2016 Texas Supreme Court pipeline case, Heidler said that "nuisance" is a condition such as smoke, gases or odors that substantially interferes with use and enjoyment of land. "Here, the 'condition' interfering with use and enjoyment of land was cold air. But it was Mother Nature -- not TDUs -- that blew cold air onto Plaintiffs' properties," Heidler said. In a response for plaintiff Bernadine Edwards, Dallas lawyer S. Ann Saucer said the utilities are not being sued for complying with ERCOT's load shed orders but because they committed malfeasance that ERCOT did not order. "TDUs are not being sued because they failed to deliver uninterrupted adequate power but because they, either intentionally or via gross negligence, created part of the power shortfall, told untruths to their customers, and then deprived their misled customers of power for undue stretches of time rather than roll blackouts, with catastrophic results including deaths," said Saucer, who is with Nachawati Law Group. The utility companies are being supported with briefs from the industry and other business groups." "Texas' grid could connect to New Mexico if company's request is approved, " Dallas Morning News' Philip Jankowski -- "A small regional electric cooperative in far West Texas is seeking to connect portions of New Mexico to Texas' self-contained power grid. The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative, a rural electric retail company that owns thousands of miles of power lines across 18 Texas counties, is not wholly part of ERCOT, but it wants to be. Last month, the power provider petitioned the federal regulatory body overseeing the nation's electric grid and asked regulators to determine whether connecting their customers to ERCOT, including homes and businesses in two New Mexico counties, would trigger federal regulation. Texas has maintained an energy grid largely disconnected from the rest of the country. This isolation has allowed the state to avoid federal regulation, which many lawmakers, including Gov. Greg Abbott , have touted as an economic advantage contributing to the state's sustained economic growth. The electric cooperative argues allowing ERCOT power to cross state lines would not trigger federal regulation because all the large electric transmission lines they own are in Texas. The electricity that crosses state lines is transmitted on smaller distribution lines like those connected to homes and small businesses across Texas. They argue that the Federal Electric Regulatory Commission does not have jurisdiction over those lines. The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative's CEO Theresa Quiroz said in an interview that allowing the co-op to connect its remaining customers to ERCOT was "critical" to the organization's ability to meet future power needs in its coverage area. "We have a lot of entities inquiring of us whether we have the capacity and ability to serve them," Quiroz said. "We have an obligation to serve them. However, we can't do it with what we have in place. At this point, we are having to turn away customers, and we don't want to do that." No hearings have been scheduled in the case, but the Rio Grande Electric Cooperative asked federal regulators to issue a ruling by Feb. 24." ($) "Medicaid whistleblowers awarded $37 million from state in 13-year-old case against Xeroz, " Austin American-Statesman's John C. Moritz -- "The state of Texas has been ordered to pay three whistleblowers more than $37 million in a 13-year-old case relating to Medicaid fraud alleging improper billings by dentists and corporate dental chains. State District Judge Maya Guerra Gamble of Travis County on Dec. 19 handed down the award, which amounts to 17.5% of a record 2019 settlement of more than $200 million the state reached with Xerox State Healthcare, formerly known as ACS State Healthcare. According to a news release from the lawyers who reresented whistleblowers Alexandra Alvarez , Joshua LaFountain and Christine Ellis , the state had settled the lawsuit but balked at paying a percentage of the award proceeds to those who had brought the allegations to light as required by a provision in a state fraud prevention law. "Because of these three courageous whistleblowers, the State of Texas ultimately showed how Xerox/ACS, Inc. deeply compromised the integrity of the Texas Medicaid program," attorneyJames "Rusty" Tucker said in the release. "Their efforts to set the record straight on the practices of corporate dental practices, led to a historic recovery by the state and will help protect taxpayers going forward." The lawyers said Ellis, an orthodontist, had observed what she believed to be fraud in orthodontic billings under the state's Medicaid program and reported it to state authorities and to a congressional committee. Ellis, Alvarez and LaFountain each filed a lawsuit in 2012 making the allegations under the Texas Medicaid Fraud Prevention Act. The act contains a provision that whistleblowers are entitled to a share of any recovery resulting from their suits, according to the lawyers. The Texas attorney general's office intervened in the lawsuits and later filed its own legal action against Xerox. When Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton February 2019, his office said in a news release that Xerox and its affiliated companies "rubber-stamped orthodontic prior authorization requests" without properly reviewing each one. Under state law, only requests that meet "strict Medicaid program requirements" are allowable under Medicaid. "Misconduct by employees of Xerox and its related companies compromised the integrity of the Medicaid program - the very program Texas hired the Xerox defendants to safeguard through the administration of a proper prior authorization review," Paxton said in the 2019 release, making no mention of the whistleblowers who originally raised the allegations. "We're proud of this recovery of taxpayer money. My office is committed to ensuring that Medicaid dollars are preserved for those who need it most."" ($) BUSINESS NEWS "Inside high-pressure sales culture of Texas' solar energy boom, " San Antonio Express-News'Sara DiNatale -- "Ian Atkinson began with a question: What should you look for when you're out knocking on doors early in the day, searching for customers? "No. 1: senior citizens," he said. Atkinson was standing at a dry-erase board in an office building in Arlington, leading a training session for solar energy sales agents. Sales of residential rooftop solar systems had shot up across Texas after . Atkinson's students wanted to get in on the action. As one of the trainees recorded video of the session, Atkinson recited tips for spotting prospects and closing deals. He ticked off telltale signs of an elderly person's home: a Buick or Oldsmobile in the driveway, neat lawns decorated with pinwheels or garden gnomes. People with gewgaws in the yard "always buy stuff," he said. He exhorted the agents to "kneel for the deal" -- get down on eye-level with a senior seated in a recliner. Speak slowly. Answer questions patiently. Pretend you're talking to your grandparents. "They might be on a fixed income where it's only Social Security or retirement," Atkinson can be heard saying, "but they usually have passing credit." Door-to-door sales agents were the engine behind the explosive growth of residential solar across Texas -- and they're blamed for . Some of them promised customers big energy savings that never materialized, rebates that didn't exist and tax benefits they didn't necessarily qualify for, a found. Sales agents also collected big commissions that were rarely disclosed to the homeowners who wound up paying them. Customers who signed for no-money-down loans to finance solar systems took salespeople at their word that the rooftop panels would generate all their home's power needs, slashing their electric bills to zero. When that proved untrue, customers were left with loan payments stretching out for decades -- on top of their utility bills. Many systems worked poorly or not at all because of flawed design or shoddy workmanship. These conclusions emerge from dozens of interviews and a review of court records, written sales training materials, training videos, agents' group text threads, inspection reports and other documents. In addition, the Express-News reviewed more than 1,000 complaints about home solar systems filed with the Texas Office of the Attorney General from January 2023 through April 2024. In 83% of those cases, customers said they'd been misled by salespeople. The victims were often elderly, ill and disabled or had limited command of English." ($) TRUMP ADMINISTRATION "Trump's 'border czar' has ties to Texas. Here's what to know, " Houston Chronicle'sBenjamin Wermund -- "The man President-elect Donald Trump tapped to oversee mass deportations of Gov.Greg Abbott who developed some of his hardline stances while working as an immigration enforcement officer in Texas. Tom Homan has vowed to launch what Trump has cast as the "largest deportation operation in the history of our country" that will start day one of his presidency. "Let me be clear: there is going to be a mass deportation because we just finished a mass illegal immigration crisis on the border," Homan said during a trip to the border with Abbott in November. He added the administration was "already planning" the operation. "We're not waiting until January to secure this nation at the highest levels this nation has ever seen," Homan said. Homan has yet to lay out many details. But experts say it would be expensive and next to impossible to carry out mass removals under the current immigration system. Homan, who worked as a New York police officer before climbing the ranks of federal immigration enforcement, led Immigrations and Customs Enforcement during Trump's first term. He was an early proponent of separating migrant parents from their children to discourage border crossings and has cheered on Abbott's efforts to install floating barriers in the Rio Grande and line the banks with thick strands of razor wire. Trump appointed Homan in an advisory role, meaning he won't need Senate confirmation to take the job. Here's what else to know about the "border czar" set to guide Trump's border policies. Texas shaped Homan's border security views Starting in 1999, Homan oversaw investigations for the Immigration and Naturalization Service, an agency that would eventually be folded into the Department of Homeland Security, and did stints in San Antonio and Dallas. Homan said an experience working a human smuggling case in Victoria helped shape his desire for stricter immigration enforcement. In that 2003 case, a truck driver bringing migrants into the country illegally reportedly cut A/C to their tractor trailer to evade suspicion at a border checkpoint. In the stifling heat, 19 migrants died. Homan was called to the scene, which he described in gruesome detail in a book detailing the decades he spent working for federal immigration enforcement. "If you saw what I saw that day, you would understand why I'm so emotional about border security, illegal immigration and national policy," he wrote. "Secure borders strangle human smugglers, drug traffickers and cartels... That's what this battle on the border is all about." His opposition to so-called "sanctuary cities" also has roots in Texas, when his agency arrested undocumented immigrants in a raid on Austin nightclubs suspected to be involved in drug dealing and prostitution in the early 2000s. Local police were uncomfortable assisting federal immigration authorities, he wrote in the book, and later the chief, whom he did not name, said the department would not be helping with immigration enforcement. "I guess this was a sign of things to come in liberal cities," he wrote, "pandering to certain voter constituencies while violating their oath and abandoning the most vulnerable in immigrant communities and the very citizens they're supposed to protect." Homan's work in Texas put him on the radar of the director of the then-newly formed Immigration and Customs Enforcement, and eventually led him to Washington. During the Obama administration, Homan oversaw deportations when they ramped up to more than 2.5 million -- setting records and leading immigrant groups to derisively refer to Barack Obama as "Deporter in Chief."" * **TOP NEWS**

"**Texas Supreme Court to review Winter Storm Uri claims against utility companies,** " Texas Lawbook's **Janet Elliott** -- "With state electric grid operators eyeing the potential for extreme cold this winter, the [Texas Supreme Court ](https://substack.com/redirect/5d39aee4-1840-4620-bd9b-a108488d874e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)has agreed to hear a petition from transmission and distribution utility companies seeking dismissal of gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims over devastating winter storm [blackouts](https://substack.com/redirect/3438336e-a69d-4105-a173-b4f9307f9d66?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) in February 2021.

Justices will hear arguments Feb. 19, four years after [Winter Storm Uri caused widespread, prolonged power outages](https://substack.com/redirect/743d416b-49e8-4a96-8f31-e0de2b8c171c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) resulting in deaths and property damage.

The court will review an [April decision by a three-judge panel of the Fourteenth Court of Appeals in Houston](https://substack.com/redirect/c8c1c00f-46cd-4e0e-a4ef-2497db36a8ce?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that more than 15,000 plaintiffs may move forward with certain claims in their wrongful death, personal injury and other Uri-related lawsuits seeking billions of dollars in damages.

The Fourteenth Court said the utility companies were entitled to dismissal of the plaintiffs' claims for negligence because Texas law does not impose common-law negligence duties associated with emergency power interruptions, ensuring adequate generation or warning of anticipated power outages.

[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $70/yr](https://substack.com/redirect/43598040-d795-4f7e-aa04-bddcc66f19fa?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
But the court of appeals said that the tariff, which is essentially the state-law mandated contract between customers and the transmission and distribution companies, does allow for gross negligence and intentional nuisance claims.

More than 200 lawsuits, consolidated into multidistrict litigation before Harris County MDL Court Judge **Sylvia Matthews** , allege that mismanagement by companies including Dallas-based Oncor Electric Delivery, CenterPoint Energy in Houston and American Electric Power constituted an intentional nuisance.

Uri was a perfect storm for the electric grid that serves most of Texas as plunging temperatures sparked record high demand amid a severely reduced energy supply, causing the grid to become unbalanced. Beginning at 1:25 a.m. on Feb. 15, 2021, [ERCOT began ordering](https://substack.com/redirect/beac3a69-43b2-43c9-a3d7-06d8d0e76030?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) the utilities to cut service to set amounts of customer demand to prevent the grid from collapsing.

Millions of Texans lost heat and power for several days as the weather remained brutally cold. News reports have attributed nearly 250 deaths to the disaster.

The transmission and distribution companies say they could be the only category of the many entities involved in the crisis to have a legal duty to deliver electricity after court rulings in other Uri-related cases went in favor of natural gas providers, power generators, retail electric providers and ERCOT defendants.

Michael A. Heidler, an appellate partner at Vinson & Elkins, said in the companies' petition for review that the Supreme Court should bar the plaintiffs' claims before significant resources are spent adjudicating "15,000 claimants' baseless complaints."

"In these exigent circumstances, no court has imposed common-law duties on TDUs [transmission and distribution utilities] to supply electricity to any particular customers for any particular duration. Established Texas law confirms that courts should not impose such a duty in these circumstances. Plaintiffs' gross-negligence claim should be dismissed," Heidler wrote.

He said the companies did not create a nuisance by shedding load in response to ERCOT's orders. Citing a 2016 Texas Supreme Court pipeline case, Heidler said that "nuisance" is a condition such as smoke, gases or odors that substantially interferes with use and enjoyment of land.

"Here, the 'condition' interfering with use and enjoyment of land was cold air. But it was Mother Nature -- not TDUs -- that blew cold air onto Plaintiffs' properties," Heidler said.

In a response for plaintiff Bernadine Edwards, Dallas lawyer S. Ann Saucer said the utilities are not being sued for complying with ERCOT's load shed orders but because they committed malfeasance that ERCOT did not order.

"TDUs are not being sued because they failed to deliver uninterrupted adequate power but because they, either intentionally or via gross negligence, created part of the power shortfall, told untruths to their customers, and then deprived their misled customers of power for undue stretches of time rather than roll blackouts, with catastrophic results including deaths," said Saucer, who is with Nachawati Law Group.

The utility companies are being supported with briefs from the industry and other business groups." [Texas Lawbook](https://substack.com/redirect/10a8076c-868b-4b9d-b665-1a27b8716b75?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)

"**Texas' grid could connect to New Mexico if company's request is approved,** " Dallas Morning News' **Philip Jankowski** -- "A small regional electric cooperative in far West Texas is seeking to connect portions of New Mexico to Texas' self-contained power grid.

The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative, a rural electric retail company that owns thousands of miles of power lines across 18 Texas counties, is not wholly part of ERCOT, but it wants to be.

Last month, the power provider petitioned the federal regulatory body overseeing the nation's electric grid and asked regulators to determine whether connecting their customers to ERCOT, including homes and businesses in two New Mexico counties, would trigger federal regulation.

Texas has maintained an energy grid largely disconnected from the rest of the country. This isolation has allowed the state to avoid federal regulation, which many lawmakers, including Gov. **Greg Abbott** , have touted as an economic advantage contributing to the state's sustained economic growth.

The electric cooperative argues allowing ERCOT power to cross state lines would not trigger federal regulation because all the large electric transmission lines they own are in Texas. The electricity that crosses state lines is transmitted on smaller distribution lines like those connected to homes and small businesses across Texas. They argue that the Federal Electric Regulatory Commission does not have jurisdiction over those lines.

The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative's CEO **Theresa Quiroz** said in an interview that allowing the co-op to connect its remaining customers to ERCOT was "critical" to the organization's ability to meet future power needs in its coverage area.

"We have a lot of entities inquiring of us whether we have the capacity and ability to serve them," Quiroz said. "We have an obligation to serve them. However, we can't do it with what we have in place. At this point, we are having to turn away customers, and we don't want to do that."

No hearings have been scheduled in the case, but the Rio Grande Electric Cooperative asked federal regulators to issue a ruling by Feb. 24." [DMN](https://substack.com/redirect/03eb6bee-ea4e-4e0e-9876-b24727f06988?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Medicaid whistleblowers awarded $37 million from state in 13-year-old case against Xeroz,** " Austin American-Statesman's **John C. Moritz** -- "The state of Texas has been ordered to pay three whistleblowers more than $37 million in a 13-year-old case relating to Medicaid fraud alleging improper billings by dentists and corporate dental chains.

State District Judge **Maya Guerra Gamble** of Travis County on Dec. 19 handed down the award, which amounts to 17.5% of a record 2019 settlement of more than $200 million the state reached with Xerox State Healthcare, formerly known as ACS State Healthcare.

According to a news release from the lawyers who reresented whistleblowers **Alexandra Alvarez** , **Joshua LaFountain** and **Christine Ellis** , the state had settled the lawsuit but balked at paying a percentage of the award proceeds to those who had brought the allegations to light as required by a provision in a state fraud prevention law.

"Because of these three courageous whistleblowers, the State of Texas ultimately showed how Xerox/ACS, Inc. deeply compromised the integrity of the Texas Medicaid program," attorney**James "Rusty" Tucker** said in the release. "Their efforts to set the record straight on the practices of corporate dental practices, led to a historic recovery by the state and will help protect taxpayers going forward."

The lawyers said Ellis, an orthodontist, had observed what she believed to be fraud in orthodontic billings under the state's Medicaid program and reported it to state authorities and to a congressional committee. Ellis, Alvarez and LaFountain each filed a lawsuit in 2012 making the allegations under the Texas Medicaid Fraud Prevention Act.

The act contains a provision that whistleblowers are entitled to a share of any recovery resulting from their suits, according to the lawyers. The Texas attorney general's office intervened in the lawsuits and later filed its own legal action against Xerox.

When Texas Attorney General **Ken Paxton** [announced the settlement in](https://substack.com/redirect/565ccc79-9bbc-4643-986b-1c16c31ada07?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) February 2019, his office said in a news release that Xerox and its affiliated companies "rubber-stamped orthodontic prior authorization requests" without properly reviewing each one. Under state law, only requests that meet "strict Medicaid program requirements" are allowable under Medicaid.

"Misconduct by employees of Xerox and its related companies compromised the integrity of the Medicaid program - the very program Texas hired the Xerox defendants to safeguard through the administration of a proper prior authorization review," Paxton said in the 2019 release, making no mention of the whistleblowers who originally raised the allegations. "We're proud of this recovery of taxpayer money. My office is committed to ensuring that Medicaid dollars are preserved for those who need it most."" [AAS](https://substack.com/redirect/31106a18-583a-4149-99f0-59d29f054689?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

* **BUSINESS NEWS**

"**Inside high-pressure sales culture of Texas' solar energy boom,** " San Antonio Express-News'**Sara DiNatale** -- "**Ian Atkinson** began with a question: What should you look for when you're out knocking on doors early in the day, searching for customers?

"No. 1: senior citizens," he said.

Atkinson was standing at a dry-erase board in an office building in Arlington, leading a training session for solar energy sales agents. Sales of residential rooftop solar systems had shot up across Texas after [Winter Storm Uri exposed the ricketiness of the state's electric grid](https://substack.com/redirect/7dee4084-5cb2-4c42-aa75-b99b0f788a2d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Atkinson's students wanted to get in on the action.

As one of the trainees recorded video of the session, Atkinson recited tips for spotting prospects and closing deals. He ticked off telltale signs of an elderly person's home: a Buick or Oldsmobile in the driveway, neat lawns decorated with pinwheels or garden gnomes.

People with gewgaws in the yard "always buy stuff," he said.

He exhorted the agents to "kneel for the deal" -- get down on eye-level with a senior seated in a recliner. Speak slowly. Answer questions patiently. Pretend you're talking to your grandparents.

"They might be on a fixed income where it's only Social Security or retirement," Atkinson can be heard saying, "but they usually have passing credit."

Door-to-door sales agents were the engine behind the explosive growth of residential solar across Texas -- and they're blamed for [many of its excesses](https://substack.com/redirect/e15d82b5-9649-404f-8b92-5681f6501ef9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Some of them promised customers big energy savings that never materialized, rebates that didn't exist and tax benefits they didn't necessarily qualify for, a [San Antonio Express-News investigation](https://substack.com/redirect/e15d82b5-9649-404f-8b92-5681f6501ef9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) found.

Sales agents also collected big commissions that were rarely disclosed to the homeowners who wound up paying them.

Customers who signed for no-money-down loans to finance solar systems took salespeople at their word that the rooftop panels would generate all their home's power needs, slashing their electric bills to zero. When that proved untrue, customers were left with loan payments stretching out for decades -- on top of their utility bills.

Many systems worked poorly or not at all because of flawed design or shoddy workmanship.

These conclusions emerge from dozens of interviews and a review of court records, written sales training materials, training videos, agents' group text threads, inspection reports and other documents. In addition, the Express-News reviewed more than 1,000 complaints about home solar systems filed with the Texas Office of the Attorney General from January 2023 through April 2024.

In 83% of those cases, customers said they'd been misled by salespeople. The victims were often elderly, ill and disabled or had limited command of English." [SAEN](https://substack.com/redirect/7d40a0ee-7dc1-489d-94bf-267952c8ad2c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

* **TRUMP ADMINISTRATION**

"**Trump's 'border czar' has ties to Texas. Here's what to know,** " Houston Chronicle's**Benjamin Wermund** -- "The man President-elect **Donald Trump** tapped to oversee mass deportations [is a close ally](https://substack.com/redirect/221ca940-a65f-48b7-8f68-db2604deee05?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) of Gov.**Greg Abbott** who developed some of his hardline stances while working as an immigration enforcement officer in Texas.

**Tom Homan** has vowed to launch what Trump has cast as the "largest deportation operation in the history of our country" that will start day one of his presidency.

"Let me be clear: there is going to be a mass deportation because we just finished a mass illegal immigration crisis on the border," Homan said during a trip to the border with Abbott in November. He added the administration was "already planning" the operation.

"We're not waiting until January to secure this nation at the highest levels this nation has ever seen," Homan said.

Homan has yet to lay out many details. But experts say it would be expensive and next to impossible to carry out mass removals under the current immigration system.

Homan, who worked as a New York police officer before climbing the ranks of federal immigration enforcement, led Immigrations and Customs Enforcement during Trump's first term. He was an early proponent of separating migrant parents from their children to discourage border crossings and has cheered on Abbott's efforts to install floating barriers in the Rio Grande and line the banks with thick strands of razor wire.

Trump appointed Homan in an advisory role, meaning he won't need Senate confirmation to take the job.

Here's what else to know about the "border czar" set to guide Trump's border policies.

**Texas shaped Homan's border security views**

Starting in 1999, Homan oversaw investigations for the Immigration and Naturalization Service, an agency that would eventually be folded into the Department of Homeland Security, and did stints in San Antonio and Dallas.

Homan said an experience working a human smuggling case in Victoria helped shape his desire for stricter immigration enforcement.

In that 2003 case, a truck driver bringing migrants into the country illegally reportedly cut A/C to their tractor trailer to evade suspicion at a border checkpoint. In the stifling heat, 19 migrants died.

Homan was called to the scene, which he described in gruesome detail in a book detailing the decades he spent working for federal immigration enforcement.

"If you saw what I saw that day, you would understand why I'm so emotional about border security, illegal immigration and national policy," he wrote. "Secure borders strangle human smugglers, drug traffickers and cartels... That's what this battle on the border is all about."

His opposition to so-called "sanctuary cities" also has roots in Texas, when his agency arrested undocumented immigrants in a raid on Austin nightclubs suspected to be involved in drug dealing and prostitution in the early 2000s.

Local police were uncomfortable assisting federal immigration authorities, he wrote in the book, and later the chief, whom he did not name, said the department would not be helping with immigration enforcement.

"I guess this was a sign of things to come in liberal cities," he wrote, "pandering to certain voter constituencies while violating their oath and abandoning the most vulnerable in immigrant communities and the very citizens they're supposed to protect."

Homan's work in Texas put him on the radar of the director of the then-newly formed Immigration and Customs Enforcement, and eventually led him to Washington.

During the Obama administration, Homan oversaw deportations when they ramped up to more than 2.5 million -- setting records and leading immigrant groups to derisively refer to Barack Obama as "Deporter in Chief."" [Houston Chronicle](https://substack.com/redirect/af7b3511-3567-43a4-853a-f4a808a1f63b?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)
